title: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
summary: Two networks in competition: Generator creates fake samples, Discriminator distinguishes real from fake. Minimax game converges (ideally) when Generator produces samples indistinguishable from real data. Sharp outputs but unstable training.
key_insights: Generator $G$: noise $z \to$ fake sample. Discriminator $D$: sample $\to$ real/fake probability.
At equilibrium: $D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere (can't tell real from fake). $G$ has learned the data distribution.
Mode collapse: $G$ learns to produce only a few samples that fool $D$. Ignores diversity. Major practical problem.
Training is UNSTABLE: $G$ and $D$ must be balanced. If $D$ is too strong: $G$ gets zero gradient. If $D$ is too weak: $G$ gets no signal.
Wasserstein GAN (WGAN): replace JS divergence with Wasserstein distance. Smoother gradients, more stable training.

mathematical_core.objective: $$\min_G \max_D \; V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
mathematical_core.wasserstein: $$W(p, q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p,q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\|x - y\|]$$ Earth mover's distance. Provides gradients even when distributions don't overlap.

derivations.title: Why GANs implicitly minimize JS divergence
derivations.consequence: GAN training = minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence. Problem: when $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$ don't overlap, $D_{JS}$ is constant → zero gradient for $G$. This is why WGAN uses Wasserstein distance instead.

related: DIFFUSION.BASIC — alternative generative approach, more stable
AUTOENCODER.VAE — different generative model, blurrier but stable

notes: GANs historically important but largely superseded by diffusion models for image generation. Still relevant for style transfer and domain adaptation.
